title: Twist, Lick, Dunk: Oreo Cookie Day!
story: Every March 6, cookie lovers across America celebrate National Oreo Cookie Day by honoring the world's favorite sandwich cookie! People share their best Oreo-eating techniques - some twist them apart to lick the cream first, others dunk them in milk until they're perfectly soft, and brave souls bite right through the whole cookie. Bakeries create special Oreo-flavored treats like milkshakes, cheesecakes, and ice cream sundaes. Social media fills with photos of creative Oreo recipes and messy milk mustaches. Some families even have Oreo-eating contests to see who can finish a sleeve of cookies the fastest without making a mess!
activity_question: What are some popular ways people eat Oreos on National Oreo Cookie Day?
activity_choices: Twist apart and lick the cream, dunk in milk, or bite through whole
Only eat them frozen with a fork and knife
Blend them into smoothies with vegetables
Toast them in the oven until they're crispy
activity_answer: Twist apart and lick the cream, dunk in milk, or bite through whole
bonus_fact: The original Oreo cookie was first sold in 1912, making it over 110 years old!

title: White Chocolate Cheesecake Gets Its Day
story: Creamy white chocolate meets rich cheesecake in the most delicious holiday ever! On March 6, people across the country celebrate National White Chocolate Cheesecake Day by baking, buying, or devouring this dreamy dessert. Bakeries create special white chocolate cheesecake flavors, while home bakers experiment with recipes featuring white chocolate chips, drizzles, and crusts. Social media fills with photos of perfectly sliced pieces topped with berries or whipped cream. Some people host cheesecake parties where everyone brings a different variety to share. Whether you prefer New York style or no-bake versions, this sweet holiday gives everyone the perfect excuse to indulge in double the decadence!
activity_question: What do bakeries often create on National White Chocolate Cheesecake Day?
follow_up_question: What creative white chocolate cheesecake flavor would you invent?

title: Frozen Food Gets Its Cool Day
story: Every March 6, people celebrate National Frozen Food Day by appreciating all the icy treats and meals sitting in their freezers! Families might cook frozen pizza for dinner, enjoy ice cream for dessert, or try a new frozen meal they've never tasted before. Some people share photos of their favorite frozen foods on social media, while others use this day to stock up on frozen fruits and vegetables. It's a perfect time to remember how frozen foods help busy families eat quickly and keep food fresh for months!
activity_question: What do some people share on social media during National Frozen Food Day?
activity_choices: Photos of their favorite frozen foods
activity_answer: Photos of their favorite frozen foods
bonus_fact: The first frozen foods were sold in grocery stores in 1930!

title: Ghana's Freedom Day Celebration
story: Every March 6, Ghana celebrates its independence with huge parades, colorful traditional clothing, and dancing in the streets! People wave red, gold, and green flags while drummers play exciting rhythms. Families gather to eat special foods and remember when Ghana became the first African country to gain independence from colonial rule in 1957.
activity_question: What colors are on Ghana's flag that people wave during Independence Day?
bonus_fact: Ghana was the first African country to gain independence from colonial rule!

title: Paella Party Time!
story: Every March 6, food lovers across America celebrate National Spanish Paella Day by cooking up this amazing rice dish! Paella comes from Spain and gets cooked in a huge, flat pan called a paellera. People gather with family and friends to make paella together, adding ingredients like chicken, seafood, vegetables, and special yellow spice called saffron. Restaurants serve special paella meals, and home cooks share their colorful creations on social media. It's the perfect day to try this delicious dish that brings people together!
activity_question: What is the special flat pan used to cook paella called?
bonus_fact: Traditional paella was first made by farmers using whatever ingredients they had nearby!

title: The World's Loudest Human Shout
story: Susan Birmingham opened her mouth and created history with the most powerful human shout ever officially recorded! Back in 1982, on March 6, she let out an incredible yell that measured 120 decibels. To understand how loud that is, imagine standing next to a chainsaw or a rock concert - that's the same volume level! Her amazing vocal achievement was carefully measured and documented, making her the official record holder for the loudest human shout. Scientists were amazed that a person could produce such powerful sound waves using only their voice. This remarkable feat shows just how incredible the human body can be when pushed to its limits.
activity_question: What year did Susan Birmingham set her shouting record?
bonus_fact: Normal conversation is only about 60 decibels - half as loud as Susan's record shout!

title: Ancient Ocean Message Finally Found
story: A beach walker in Western Australia made an incredible discovery that would rewrite history books! Back on March 6, 2018, someone found the world's oldest message in a bottle, sitting quietly in the sand after an amazing journey. This special bottle had been thrown from a German ship called Paula way back in 1886, making it over a century old when it was finally discovered. The message inside was still readable after all those years floating in the ocean and sitting on beaches. Scientists were amazed that paper could survive so long in a bottle, proving that sometimes the most unexpected treasures wash up on shore when we least expect them.
activity_question: What was the name of the German ship that threw the bottle?
follow_up_question: What other old treasures might be hiding on beaches around the world?

title: The Bouncy Blob That Became a Toy
story: A strange, stretchy substance bounced its way into American toy stores when Silly Putty went on sale across the United States in 1950. This weird, wonderful material had been created by accident during World War Two when scientists were trying to make synthetic rubber. Instead, they got something that could bounce, stretch, and copy pictures from comic books! On March 6 of that year, kids everywhere could finally buy their own blob of this magical putty. It came in a plastic egg, which made it even more special. Children discovered they could roll it into a ball, flatten it like pancake batter, or press it onto newspaper to transfer the ink. The accidental invention became one of the most beloved toys ever made.
activity_question: What year did Silly Putty go on sale across the United States?
bonus_fact: Silly Putty can stretch longer than a school bus before breaking!

title: The Trampoline Gets Its Patent
story: Bouncing high into the air became much safer thanks to George Nissen of Cedar Rapids, Iowa! Back on March 6, 1945, he received a patent for the first modern trampoline. Nissen had been working on his bouncing invention for years, inspired by circus safety nets. His trampoline used a strong canvas bed stretched over a steel frame with springs. This clever design let people bounce safely for fun and exercise. Soon trampolines spread to gyms, backyards, and Olympic training centers around the world. Remember, trampolines can be dangerous without proper safety equipment and adult supervision, so always follow safety rules when bouncing!
activity_question: What city was George Nissen from?
bonus_fact: The word 'trampoline' comes from the Spanish word 'trampolin' meaning diving board.

title: The Deep-Diving Champion
story: A future champion took his first breath in Volgograd, Russia, when Alexey Molchanov was born on March 6, 1987. This Russian freediver would grow up to become one of the most amazing underwater athletes in the world. Freediving means holding your breath and diving as deep as possible without any breathing equipment - just your lungs and determination! Molchanov trained his body and mind to stay calm underwater for incredibly long times. His dedication paid off as he went on to set an astounding 25 world records in his sport. From that day in 1987 when he was born, nobody could have predicted this baby would one day become a master of the deep blue sea.
activity_question: How many world records did Alexey Molchanov set in freediving?
bonus_fact: Freedivers can hold their breath for over 10 minutes!

title: Batman Gets His First Sidekick
story: A young acrobat swings into comic book history! Dick Grayson made his debut as Robin, Batman's first sidekick, in Detective Comics issue 38 back in 1940. This happened on March 6, the same date we celebrate today. Before Robin joined the Dark Knight, Batman fought crime alone in the shadows of Gotham City. But DC Comics decided their hero needed a partner, someone younger readers could relate to. Dick Grayson brought energy, hope, and colorful red and green costume to balance Batman's dark world. The Boy Wonder quickly became a fan favorite, proving that even superheroes work better as a team.
activity_question: In which comic book issue did Robin first appear as Batman's sidekick?
bonus_fact: Robin's costume colors were chosen to contrast with Batman's dark outfit.

title: Iron Mike's First Pro Fight
story: A young boxer named Mike Tyson stepped into the ring for his very first professional fight back in 1985, right here on March 6. The future heavyweight champion faced Hector Mercedes in Albany, New York, and what happened next was lightning fast. Tyson knocked out his opponent at 1:47 in the first round of what was supposed to be a four-round match. This explosive debut showed the world that a new boxing star was born. Years later, Tyson would become the undisputed world heavyweight champion, but it all started with this incredible knockout victory.
activity_question: Who did Mike Tyson fight in his first professional boxing match?
bonus_fact: Tyson was only 18 years old when he had this first professional fight!

title: A Star is Born in Utah
story: A future movie star took her first breath in Bountiful, Utah, changing Hollywood forever. Millicent Simmonds was born on March 6, 2003, and would grow up to become a groundbreaking actress. Born deaf, she didn't let anything stop her from pursuing her dreams of acting. Years later, she starred in the thrilling movie 'A Quiet Place', where her real-life experience with deafness made her performance incredibly powerful and authentic. Millicent shows kids everywhere that being different can be your greatest strength, and that dreams really can come true with hard work and determination.
activity_question: In which Utah city was Millicent Simmonds born?
bonus_fact: Millicent uses American Sign Language to communicate and helped teach her co-stars for the movie.

title: The Vaccine Pioneer's Birthday
story: A brilliant scientist named Özlem Türeci changed the world with her groundbreaking work on vaccines. Born in Siegen, West Germany on March 6, 1967, she would grow up to become one of the most important medical researchers of our time. Working at BioNTech, Türeci helped create the first mRNA-based vaccine for COVID-19, a revolutionary breakthrough that protected millions of people around the globe. Her innovative approach to fighting disease showed how dedication to science can truly make a difference in everyone's lives.
activity_question: Where was Özlem Türeci born?
bonus_fact: mRNA vaccines teach our cells how to make proteins that fight viruses.

title: The First Periodic Table
story: A brilliant Russian scientist named Dmitri Mendeleev had been puzzling over the chemical elements for months. He noticed patterns in how they behaved and decided to organize them in a special way. On March 6 in 1869, Mendeleev presented his groundbreaking work to the Russian Chemical Society - the very first periodic table of the elements! This amazing chart arranged all the known elements by their properties and even left empty spaces where Mendeleev predicted new elements would be discovered. His periodic table became one of the most important tools in science, helping students and scientists understand chemistry for generations to come.
activity_question: What did Dmitri Mendeleev present to the Russian Chemical Society?
activity_choices: the first periodic table of the elements
activity_answer: the first periodic table of the elements
bonus_fact: Mendeleev's table correctly predicted the properties of elements that hadn't been discovered yet!

title: The Sticky Genius Behind Superglue
story: A baby boy named Harry Coover entered the world in Newark, Delaware, destined to create one of the stickiest inventions ever! Born on March 6, 1917, this future chemist would grow up to invent Superglue, the amazing adhesive that can bond almost anything together in seconds. Harry spent his life mixing chemicals and experimenting in laboratories. His accidental discovery of super-strong glue happened while he was trying to make clear plastic gun sights during World War Two. The gooey mistake turned into a household hero that helps fix broken toys, crafts, and countless other items around the world.
activity_question: In which city and state was Harry Coover born?
bonus_fact: Superglue was first used to close wounds on soldiers during the Vietnam War!

title: The Wonder Drug Gets Its Patent
story: Felix Hoffmann had created something amazing! Back on March 6, 1899, this German scientist received a patent for aspirin at the Bayer company. Aspirin, also called acetylsalicylic acid, would become one of the world's most helpful medicines. People use it to reduce pain, fever, and swelling. What started as a chemical experiment in a lab became a medicine that millions of people rely on today.
activity_question: What is aspirin also called?
bonus_fact: Aspirin was originally made from willow tree bark!

title: Dawn Reaches the Dwarf Planet Ceres
story: A robotic explorer named Dawn made space history when it became the first spacecraft to orbit a dwarf planet! NASA's Dawn space probe successfully entered orbit around Ceres on March 6, 2015. Ceres is the largest object in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, and scientists were excited to study this mysterious world up close. Dawn had already visited another asteroid called Vesta before making its way to Ceres. The spacecraft carried special instruments to take pictures and measure what Ceres was made of, helping us learn more about the early solar system.
activity_question: What was the name of the NASA space probe that entered orbit around Ceres?
bonus_fact: Ceres is so big that it contains about one-third of all asteroid belt mass!

title: LeBron Passes Jordan in NBA History
story: Basketball superstar LeBron James made history while playing for the Los Angeles Lakers. During the second quarter of a game against the Denver Nuggets, he scored his 32,293rd career point. This amazing achievement moved him past the legendary Michael Jordan into fourth place on the NBA's all-time scoring list. The milestone happened on March 6, 2019, though the Lakers lost the game with a final score of 115-99. LeBron had worked for years to reach this incredible moment in basketball history.
activity_question: Which team was LeBron James playing for when he passed Michael Jordan on the scoring list?
follow_up_question: Can you name another famous basketball player who might be higher on the all-time scoring list?

title: Youngest Boxing Champion Ever
story: A teenage boxer made history that would shock the sports world! Wilfred Benítez, just seventeen years old, stepped into the ring in San Juan, Puerto Rico on March 6, 1976. He faced Antonio Cervantes, the experienced champion from Colombia who was defending his WBA light welterweight title for the tenth time. After fifteen intense rounds, the judges made their decision. In a split decision victory, young Benítez defeated the seasoned champion and became the youngest world boxing champion ever. The Puerto Rican teenager's incredible achievement proved that age doesn't always determine who wins in the ring.
activity_question: How old was Wilfred Benítez when he became world champion?
bonus_fact: Boxing matches are divided into rounds, with each round lasting three minutes.

title: Basketball's Wildest Scoring Battle
story: Two basketball teams couldn't stop scoring! Back on March 6, 1982, the San Antonio Spurs and Milwaukee Bucks played one of the most exciting games in NBA history. The players kept making shot after shot, and neither team could pull ahead for good. When regular time ended, they were still tied. They played one overtime period, then another, then a third! Finally, after three extra periods of non-stop action, San Antonio won with a final score of 171-166. That's a lot of points for both teams! The fans who stayed until the very end got to see basketball history.
activity_question: What was the final score when San Antonio beat Milwaukee?
follow_up_question: How many overtime periods did this game have?

title: Soviet Spacecraft Meets Famous Comet
story: A Soviet spacecraft named Vega 1 zoomed past one of the most famous comets in our solar system! Back in 1986, on March 6, this robotic explorer flew incredibly close to Halley's Comet, passing at a distance of 8,889 kilometers. Halley's Comet only visits Earth's neighborhood once every 76 years, making this flyby extra special. The spacecraft took amazing photos and collected valuable data about the comet's icy surface and glowing tail. Scientists around the world were thrilled to learn new secrets about this ancient visitor from the outer reaches of space.
activity_question: What was the name of the Soviet spacecraft that flew past Halley's Comet?
bonus_fact: Halley's Comet won't return until 2061!

title: Space Signal Travels Record Distance
story: A tiny spacecraft called Pioneer IV made history by sending the farthest radio signal ever heard from space! Back on March 6, 1959, this brave little probe was zooming through the darkness between Earth and the Moon. Scientists on Earth were amazed when they could still hear Pioneer IV's radio beeps from an incredible distance away. The spacecraft had traveled far beyond what anyone thought possible for radio signals to reach back home. Pioneer IV kept talking to Earth from farther than any human-made object had ever communicated before, proving that space exploration could reach amazing new distances.
activity_question: What was the name of the spacecraft that sent the farthest radio signal?
bonus_fact: Pioneer IV was only about the size of a small beach ball!

title: Future Astronaut Born in Oklahoma
story: A baby boy named Gordon Cooper entered the world in Shawnee, Oklahoma, back in 1927 on March 6. This little one would grow up to become something amazing - an American aerospace engineer, test pilot, and astronaut! Cooper would later zoom through space on two incredible missions: Mercury-Atlas 9 and Gemini 5. From his small-town beginnings in Oklahoma, he reached for the stars and made it all the way to space, showing that dreams really can take you anywhere in the universe.
activity_question: What were the names of the two space missions Gordon Cooper flew on?
bonus_fact: Cooper was one of the original seven Mercury astronauts chosen by NASA.

title: Lost Warship Found After Decades
story: Deep beneath the waves of Australia's Coral Sea, explorers made an amazing discovery on March 6, 2018. They found the USS Lexington, a massive American aircraft carrier that had been missing for decades. This mighty warship was lost during the Battle of the Coral Sea back in 1942, during World War II. The ship had been resting on the ocean floor, hidden from the world above. Finding the Lexington was like solving a giant underwater puzzle that had stumped people for years. The discovery helped us learn more about this important piece of history.
activity_question: In what year was the USS Lexington rediscovered?
bonus_fact: The Lexington was nicknamed 'Lady Lex' by her crew members.

title: The One-Armed Baseball Star
story: Pete Gray swung his bat with incredible determination, proving that nothing could stop his baseball dreams. Born on March 6, 1915, in Nanticoke, Pennsylvania, Pete became one of only three one-armed players to ever make it to Major League Baseball. Despite losing his right arm in a childhood accident, he developed amazing skills that caught the attention of the St. Louis Browns. Pete could catch a fly ball, quickly tuck his glove under his arm stub, grab the ball, and throw it back faster than many two-armed players. His inspiring story shows that with hard work and creativity, people can overcome any challenge to achieve their goals.
activity_question: Which Major League Baseball team did Pete Gray play for?
bonus_fact: Pete could field a ball and throw it back in just one smooth motion!

title: A Creative Rapper is Born
story: Music fans around the world celebrate creativity every day, but March 6 holds special meaning for hip-hop lovers. Back in 1991, Tyler Okonma was born in Ladera Heights, California on this very date. You probably know him better as Tyler, the Creator! This talented rapper and record producer would grow up to make amazing albums like Flower Boy and Igor. His unique style and creative vision have inspired countless young artists. From his California birthplace to stages worldwide, Tyler has shown that being different and creative can lead to incredible success in music.
activity_question: What is Tyler, the Creator's real name?
bonus_fact: Tyler, the Creator also designs his own album covers and music videos!

title: Marathon Legend Born
story: A future running champion took his first breath in London, England on March 6, 1931. Ed Whitlock would grow up to become one of the most amazing marathon runners ever. This Canadian athlete made history when he became the first person over seventy years old to run a marathon in less than three hours, finishing in 2:59:10 back in 2003. Even as he got older, Ed kept running and inspiring people around the world. He showed everyone that age is just a number when you have determination and love what you do.
activity_question: What time did Ed Whitlock finish his historic marathon in 2003?
follow_up_question: What other sports require similar endurance to marathon running?

title: Moon Landing Goes Sideways
story: A spacecraft named Athena tumbled onto its side during its moon landing on March 6, 2025! The robotic explorer, built by a company called Intuitive Machines in Texas, was carrying a special NASA ice drill designed to search for frozen water on the lunar surface. Unfortunately, when Athena touched down, it tipped over instead of landing upright like planned. This sideways landing meant the mission had to end earlier than scientists hoped. Even though things didn't go perfectly, the brave little spacecraft still made it all the way to the Moon, which is an incredible journey of about 240,000 miles from Earth.
activity_question: What was the name of the spacecraft that landed on the Moon in 2025?
follow_up_question: What other space missions have tried to find water on the Moon?

title: The Hunt for Secret Codes
story: Enemy submarines lurked beneath the ocean waves, carrying secret code machines that could change the war. British and Canadian sailors made a daring move on March 6, 1944, boarding an abandoned German U-boat called U-774. They desperately searched for the famous Enigma machine, which could decode enemy messages and help win battles. The brave sailors explored every corner of the submarine, hoping to find this incredible device. Unfortunately, their efforts to tow the heavy submarine back to safety failed completely. With no other choice, they had to sink the U-boat themselves, watching their prize disappear beneath the dark waters.
activity_question: What were the sailors searching for on the German submarine?
bonus_fact: Enigma machines looked like typewriters but created unbreakable secret codes for military messages.

title: The Boy Who Mapped Sunlight
story: A curious mind was born in Bavaria on March 6, 1787, destined to unlock the secrets of light itself. Joseph von Fraunhofer grew up to become a brilliant German physicist and optical lens maker who would change how we understand the Sun forever. He carefully studied the Sun's spectrum, discovering dark lines that revealed what elements exist in our star. His greatest invention was the spectroscope, a special instrument that splits light into its rainbow colors so scientists can analyze it. This amazing tool helped astronomers learn about distant stars and planets by examining their light, opening up entirely new ways to explore the universe from Earth.
activity_question: What instrument did Joseph von Fraunhofer invent to split light into colors?
bonus_fact: Fraunhofer's dark lines in sunlight are still called 'Fraunhofer lines' by scientists today.

title: The Manuscript That Went Up in Smoke
story: A maid accidentally burned one of history's most important books before anyone could read it! Back on March 6, 1835, Thomas Carlyle had just finished writing Volume One of 'The French Revolution: A History.' But disaster struck when a maid mistook his precious manuscript for scrap paper and tossed it into the fireplace to start a fire. Instead of giving up, the determined author rolled up his sleeves and rewrote the entire volume from memory.
activity_question: What did the maid use Carlyle's manuscript for?
follow_up_question: How do you think Carlyle felt when he discovered his manuscript was gone?

title: The Sneaky Peat Barge Trick
story: A clever military trick fooled an entire city back in 1590! Dutch and English soldiers, led by Maurice of Nassau, wanted to capture the heavily protected city of Breda. Instead of a big battle, they came up with a sneaky plan. They hid a small assault force inside a peat barge - a boat that normally carried chunks of dried plant matter for fuel. The enemy guards never suspected that soldiers were hiding inside what looked like an ordinary supply boat. On March 6 of that year, this brilliant trick worked perfectly, and they captured the city without anyone expecting it!
activity_question: What did the soldiers hide inside to capture the city?
bonus_fact: Peat was dried plant matter that people burned for heat and cooking fuel.

title: Swimming Star Born in China
story: A future swimming champion took his first breath in Zhengzhou, China! Ning Zetao was born on March 6, 1993, and would grow up to make waves in pools around the world. This talented athlete became known for his incredible speed in the water, especially in freestyle swimming. Years later, he would prove his skills by winning gold at the World Championships in the 100-meter freestyle event in 2015. His journey from a baby in Zhengzhou to a world champion swimmer shows how dreams can come true with hard work and dedication.
activity_question: In which year did Ning Zetao win gold at the World Championships in the 100-meter freestyle?
follow_up_question: What other swimming strokes do you think world champions need to practice?

title: Brazil's First Indigenous Presidential Candidate
story: A baby girl named Sônia Guajajara was born in Araribóia Indigenous Land, Brazil, marking the beginning of an extraordinary life. Born in 1974 on March 6, she would grow up to become a powerful voice for indigenous people across her country. As an activist and politician, Sônia fought tirelessly to protect indigenous rights and preserve traditional ways of life. Her dedication and courage led to a historic moment when she became the first indigenous person ever to appear on a presidential ticket in Brazil. Through her work, she showed young people everywhere that they can stand up for what they believe in and make their voices heard, no matter where they come from.
activity_question: What was historic about Sônia Guajajara's role in Brazilian politics?
activity_choices: She became the first indigenous person on a presidential ticket in Brazil
She was the youngest politician in Brazilian history
She founded the first indigenous political party
She became Brazil's first female president
activity_answer: She became the first indigenous person on a presidential ticket in Brazil
follow_up_question: What other ways can young people make their voices heard in their communities?

title: French Astronaut Born in Africa
story: A baby boy named Patrick Baudry took his first breath in Douala, Cameroon on March 6, 1946. This little one would grow up to become something amazing - a French astronaut! Years later, Patrick blasted off into space aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery on mission STS-51-G. He became the second French citizen ever to travel beyond Earth's atmosphere. His journey from being born in the bustling city of Douala to floating weightless among the stars shows how dreams can take you anywhere in the universe.
activity_question: What was the name of Patrick Baudry's space shuttle mission?
bonus_fact: Patrick trained for years to handle the incredible forces of rocket launch and weightlessness.

title: Babe Ruth's Big Contract
story: Baseball's biggest star grabbed his pen and made history! Babe Ruth signed a huge three-year contract with the New York Yankees back in 1922, right here on March 6. The deal paid him an amazing 52,000 dollars each year, making him one of the highest-paid athletes of his time. This contract helped cement Ruth's legendary status with the Yankees, where he would go on to become the most famous baseball player ever.
activity_question: How much money did Babe Ruth earn each year from his Yankees contract?
bonus_fact: Ruth was nicknamed the 'Sultan of Swat' for his powerful home runs!

title: The Beatles' Final Gift
story: Four lads from Liverpool changed music forever, and their last single proved they still had magic left. The Beatles released 'Let it Be' in the United Kingdom back in 1970, the same March 6 we celebrate today. This beautiful song became their final single as a group, with Paul McCartney's gentle piano and meaningful words about finding peace during tough times. The track showed the world that even when things get difficult, sometimes the best thing to do is just let it be and trust that everything will work out okay.
activity_question: In what year did The Beatles release 'Let it Be' in the United Kingdom?
bonus_fact: 'Let it Be' was inspired by a dream Paul had about his mother.

title: Cricket Legend's First Match
story: A young Indian batsman stepped onto the cricket field in the West Indies, ready to face some of the world's best bowlers. Sunil Gavaskar made his Test Cricket debut against the West Indies at Port-of-Spain on March 6, 1971. This marked the beginning of an incredible career that would span many years. Gavaskar went on to play in Test matches, scoring runs at an impressive average. He became famous for his batting skills and determination, earning respect from cricket fans around the world.
activity_question: Where did Sunil Gavaskar make his Test Cricket debut?
bonus_fact: Gavaskar was known for his patient batting style and strong defense against fast bowlers.

title: My Girl Hits Number One
story: Sweet harmonies filled the airwaves as The Temptations celebrated their biggest hit yet. March 6, 1965 marked the day when 'My Girl' reached number one on the Billboard Pop Chart. This beloved song was written and produced by Smokey Robinson and Ronald White, who were members of another famous group called the Miracles. The catchy tune with its memorable lyrics became one of the most popular songs of the decade, showing how talented musicians could create magic together.
activity_question: Who wrote and produced 'My Girl' for The Temptations?
bonus_fact: The Miracles and The Temptations were both part of the famous Motown record label.

title: The Comic Book Pioneer
story: A baby boy named Will Eisner entered the world in Kolomyia, Austria-Hungary, destined to change comics forever. Born on March 6, 1917, Eisner would grow up to become one of America's most important cartoonists. His most famous creation was 'The Spirit,' a masked detective who fought crime in exciting comic book adventures. Eisner didn't just draw comics - he helped invent new ways to tell stories with pictures and words together, making comics more like movies on paper.
activity_question: What was Will Eisner's most famous comic book creation?
bonus_fact: Comic book awards are called 'Eisner Awards' in his honor!

title: The Man Behind the Famous Big Nose
story: A baby boy with a destiny for adventure was born in Paris on March 6, 1619. His name was Cyrano de Bergerac, and he would grow up to become a famous French playwright and novelist. Cyrano was known for having a very large nose, which made him quite memorable. He wrote amazing stories, including one called 'Voyage to the Moon' about traveling to space long before rockets were invented. His life was so interesting that another writer named Edmond Rostand later wrote a famous play about him, making Cyrano's story live on forever.
activity_question: What was the name of Cyrano's story about traveling to space?
bonus_fact: Cyrano wrote about space travel over 200 years before the first airplane was built!

title: The Amazing Persian Calendar Genius
story: A brilliant Persian scholar named Omar Khayyam finished creating something incredible that would last for centuries. Back in 1079, right here on March 6, this amazing polymath completed the Jalali calendar. This wasn't just any ordinary calendar - it was a solar calendar with a super precise system that repeated every 33 years. Omar's clever design became the foundation for the Persian calendar that people still use today, nearly a thousand years later!
activity_question: What type of calendar did Omar Khayyam create?
bonus_fact: Omar Khayyam was also famous for writing beautiful poetry!

title: The Swedish Explorer Who Described Niagara Falls
story: A baby named Pehr Kalm was born in Ångermanland, Sweden on March 6, 1716, and he would grow up to become something amazing. This curious boy became a Swedish explorer and naturalist who traveled far from his snowy homeland. Kalm made history by writing the first scientific description of the mighty Niagara Falls, helping people around the world understand this incredible natural wonder through his careful observations and detailed notes.
activity_question: Where was Pehr Kalm born?
bonus_fact: Kalm also discovered and named many North American plants that still bear his name today.

title: Cricket's Greatest Series Victory
story: Jack Brown swung his bat with lightning speed, smashing cricket balls all around the field! Back in 1895, on March 6, England faced Australia in what many call one of the greatest cricket series ever played. Brown made history by hitting the fastest fifty runs in test cricket in just twenty-eight minutes. His incredible batting helped England win the thrilling series with a final score of three wins to two. Cricket fans still talk about this amazing match today!
activity_question: How many minutes did it take Jack Brown to hit the fastest fifty runs in test cricket?
follow_up_question: What other sports have records that seem almost impossible to break?

title: Cricket Century on Debut
story: Cricket fans witnessed something special when Bruce Taylor stepped up to bat for New Zealand against India in 1965. Playing in his very first Test match, Taylor swung his bat with confidence and skill. On March 6 that year, he reached an incredible milestone by scoring 105 runs in his debut innings. This amazing achievement made him one of the few players to score a century in their first Test match. Taylor's powerful batting helped put New Zealand in a strong position against the visiting Indian team.
activity_question: How many runs did Bruce Taylor score in his first Test cricket innings?
bonus_fact: Scoring a century on Test debut is one of cricket's rarest achievements.

title: Space Capsule Comes Home
story: A small capsule streaked through Earth's atmosphere like a shooting star, carrying two brave astronauts back home. The Soyuz 39 spacecraft had completed its mission in space and was returning to our planet on March 6, 1981. Inside the capsule, the crew prepared for landing after spending days orbiting high above Earth. The spacecraft used parachutes to slow down as it approached the ground, ensuring a safe touchdown for the astronauts inside.
activity_question: What year did the Soyuz 39 return to Earth?
bonus_fact: Soyuz spacecraft have been safely bringing astronauts home for over fifty years.

title: Jazz Guitar Legend Born
story: A baby boy named John Montgomery entered the world in Indianapolis, Indiana, and would grow up to change jazz music forever. Born on March 6, 1923, he became known as Wes Montgomery, one of the most influential jazz guitarists ever. His unique thumb-picking style created a warm, mellow sound that made other musicians stop and listen. Wes won Grammy Awards for his incredible talent and inspired countless guitarists who came after him. Though he lived only until 1968, his musical legacy continues to influence jazz players around the world today.
activity_question: What unique playing technique did Wes Montgomery use that created his distinctive sound?
bonus_fact: Wes taught himself to play guitar by listening to records and copying the sounds.

title: Pop Art Pioneer Born in London
story: Bright colors and bold images filled the art world when Pauline Boty helped create something amazing! This talented English artist was born in London on March 6, 1938, and became one of the co-founders of the Pop art movement. Pop art took everyday things like soup cans and comic books and turned them into exciting artwork. Boty painted vibrant pictures that celebrated popular culture and made art feel fun and accessible to everyone. Her creative spirit helped change how people thought about art forever.
activity_question: What art movement did Pauline Boty help co-found?
bonus_fact: Pop art made everyday objects like soup cans famous in museums!

title: J-pop Star Becomes Kindergarten Teacher
story: A future J-pop sensation took her first breath in Kashiwa, Chiba, Japan when Momoko Tsugunaga was born on March 6, 1992. This talented performer would grow up to become a beloved singer with the group Berryz Kobo, captivating fans across Japan with her voice and stage presence. But music wasn't her only passion - she also became an actress, radio personality, and even a kindergarten teacher, showing that dreams can take many wonderful forms throughout life.
activity_question: What group did Momoko Tsugunaga sing with?
bonus_fact: Many J-pop stars pursue different careers after their music days end.

title: A Supreme Star is Born
story: A future music legend took her first breath in Detroit, Michigan back in 1944. Mary Wilson was born on March 6 that year, destined to become one of the most famous voices in pop music history. She would grow up to be a founding member of The Supremes, the incredible group that gave us hits like 'Where Did Our Love Go?' The Supremes became one of the most successful girl groups ever, bringing joy to millions of fans around the world with their catchy songs and dazzling performances.
activity_question: In which city was Mary Wilson born?
bonus_fact: The Supremes had twelve number-one hits on the Billboard Hot 100 chart.

title: Chess Champion Born in Jamaica
story: A future chess champion took his first breath in St. Andrews, Jamaica on March 6, 1966. Maurice Ashley would grow up to make history by becoming the first Black Grandmaster in chess. This incredible achievement opened doors for many young players who looked like him. Ashley didn't just play chess - he also became a popular commentator, helping fans understand the exciting moves happening on the board. His journey from Jamaica to chess greatness shows that with hard work and determination, anyone can reach the top of their game.
activity_question: What historic achievement did Maurice Ashley accomplish in chess?
follow_up_question: What other chess job did Maurice Ashley have besides playing?

title: A Ballet Star is Born
story: A future ballet superstar took his first breath in Munro, Buenos Aires! Julio Bocca was born on March 6, 1967, and would grow up to become one of Argentina's most celebrated dancers. He danced with the famous American Ballet Theater and helped create Ballet Argentino. Later, he became the artistic director of Uruguay's National Ballet, sharing his love of dance with new generations of performers across South America.
activity_question: In which city was Julio Bocca born?
bonus_fact: Ballet dancers often start training when they're very young, sometimes around age six!

title: Future Olympic Champion Born
story: A future basketball star took her first breath in Peoria, Illinois! Carla McGhee was born on March 6, 1968, and would grow up to become an amazing basketball forward. She helped the University of Tennessee win NCAA Championships in 1987 and 1989. Her greatest moment came when she earned Olympic gold in 1996, representing the United States on the world's biggest sports stage.
activity_question: In which city was Carla McGhee born?
follow_up_question: What other sports require teamwork like basketball?

title: A Star is Born in South Africa
story: Beautiful voices can come from anywhere in the world! Pretty Yende was born in Piet Retief, a town in Mpumalanga, South Africa, on March 6, 1985. She would grow up to become one of the world's most celebrated operatic sopranos. Opera singers train for years to master their powerful voices and perform dramatic stories through song. Pretty's journey from a small South African town to opera stages around the globe shows that dreams can take you anywhere when you work hard and follow your passion for music.
activity_question: In which South African province was Pretty Yende born?
bonus_fact: Opera singers can project their voices to fill huge theaters without microphones!

title: A Soldier's Song Tops the Charts
story: A military song about brave soldiers climbed to the very top of America's music charts back in 1966, right here on March 6! Barry Sadler, who was actually a real Green Beret soldier himself, wrote and sang 'Ballad of the Green Berets' to honor his fellow troops. The powerful song struck a chord with listeners across the country and became the number one hit, staying at the top for many weeks. It was unusual for a military-themed song to become so popular, but Sadler's heartfelt tribute to these special forces soldiers resonated with millions of Americans during a time when many young men were serving their country.
activity_question: What was the name of Barry Sadler's hit song?
bonus_fact: Barry Sadler was one of the few recording artists who was also an active military soldier.

title: The Vanishing Ship Mystery
story: A massive naval cargo ship called the Cyclops sailed into one of the ocean's greatest mysteries. The vessel was carrying tons of heavy cargo when it vanished without a trace on March 6, 1918. The ship disappeared somewhere in the area now known as the Bermuda Triangle, between Florida, Bermuda, and Puerto Rico. No distress signal was ever sent, and despite extensive searches, neither the ship nor its crew was ever found. This puzzling disappearance became one of the most famous unsolved maritime mysteries in history.
activity_question: What was the name of the naval ship that disappeared?
bonus_fact: The Bermuda Triangle has been linked to many other mysterious disappearances over the years.

title: Ghana Breaks Free
story: Freedom bells rang across West Africa as a nation threw off colonial chains! Ghana declared its independence from the United Kingdom in 1957, right here on March 6. This former British colony called the Gold Coast became the first African nation south of the Sahara to gain independence. Kwame Nkrumah became the country's first prime minister, leading his people into a new era of self-rule and hope for the future.
activity_question: What was Ghana called when it was a British colony?
bonus_fact: Ghana's flag features red, gold, and green with a black star representing African freedom.

title: First European Eyes on Guam
story: A Portuguese ship captain named Ferdinand Magellan spotted something amazing through his telescope! Sailing across the vast Pacific Ocean in 1521, he became the first European to see the island of Guam in the western Pacific. This happened on March 6, the same date we celebrate today. Magellan was leading a Spanish expedition trying to find a new route to the Spice Islands. After months at sea, his crew was probably very excited to see land. This discovery connected two different worlds for the first time in history.
activity_question: What was Ferdinand Magellan trying to find when he spotted Guam?
activity_choices: a new route to the Spice Islands
a place to build a castle
activity_answer: a new route to the Spice Islands
bonus_fact: Guam is still a U.S. territory today, over 500 years later!

title: The Dalai Lama Gets His COVID Shot
story: A needle prick brought hope to millions around the world. The Dalai Lama, Tibet's beloved spiritual leader, received his COVID-19 vaccination in Dharamsala, India on March 6, 2021. This gentle monk, known for his wisdom and kindness, didn't just quietly get his shot. He spoke up boldly, encouraging people everywhere to get vaccinated too. His message was simple but powerful: protecting ourselves helps protect others. When one of the world's most respected religious leaders shows the way, people listen.
activity_question: Where did the Dalai Lama receive his COVID-19 vaccination?
bonus_fact: The Dalai Lama has lived in Dharamsala since fleeing Tibet in 1959.

title: Australian Town Faces Rising Waters
story: Water levels climbed dangerously high as the Murrimbidgee River swelled near Wagga Wagga, Australia. Emergency teams worked quickly to help keep everyone safe when flooding threatened the town on March 6, 2012. About nine thousand residents had to leave their homes temporarily while the river was at risk of overflowing its banks. The community came together during this challenging time, with neighbors helping neighbors pack up and find safe places to stay until the waters went down.
activity_question: In what year did the Murrimbidgee River threaten to overflow near Wagga Wagga?
bonus_fact: Wagga Wagga's name comes from an Aboriginal word meaning 'place of many crows'.

title: The Father of Ragtime Piano is Born
story: A baby's cry echoed along the Mississippi River as Ben Harney entered the world in 1871, right here on March 6. This little boy would grow up to become known as the 'Father of Ragtime Piano', creating bouncy, toe-tapping music that made people want to dance. One of his most famous songs had the funny title 'You've Been a Good Old Wagon, But You Done Broke Down'. Harney's ragtime music helped shape American music forever, bringing joy to countless listeners with its playful rhythms and catchy melodies.
activity_question: What was Ben Harney known as?
follow_up_question: Can you tap out a ragtime rhythm with your fingers?

title: Baseball Legend Born in Small Town
story: A future baseball superstar took his first breath in the small town of Lonaconing, Maryland, back in 1900 on March 6. Robert 'Lefty' Grove would grow up to become one of the greatest pitchers in baseball history. His powerful left arm helped him win the Triple Crown twice and earn the American League MVP award. Grove played for the Philadelphia Athletics and Boston Red Sox during his amazing career. He became a Hall of Fame pitcher and was selected as an All-Star multiple times, proving that big dreams can come from small places.
activity_question: In which small town was Robert 'Lefty' Grove born?
bonus_fact: Grove's nickname 'Lefty' came from his powerful left-handed pitching style.

title: The King of Western Swing is Born
story: A baby boy named Bob Wills entered the world in Limestone County, Texas, who would grow up to become the 'King of Western Swing.' Born on March 6, 1905, Bob would later form a famous band called the Texas Playboys and create music that mixed country with jazz and blues. His band's hit song 'Steel Guitar Rag' helped make western swing popular across America. Bob's energetic style and catchy tunes made people want to dance and have fun at concerts and dance halls.
activity_question: What was the name of Bob Wills' famous band?
bonus_fact: Western swing music made people dance by mixing country, jazz, and blues together.

title: A Star-Studying Pioneer
story: Stars fascinated Madge Adam from an early age, leading her to become something truly special. Born in London, England on March 6, 1912, she would grow up to make history as the first solar physicist at Oxford University's observatory. Solar physicists study our sun and how it works, learning about the giant ball of fire that gives us light and warmth. Madge spent her career watching the sun through powerful telescopes and discovering its secrets. She helped other scientists understand solar flares and sunspots better than ever before.
activity_question: Where was Madge Adam born?
bonus_fact: Solar physicists use special filters to safely observe the sun without damaging their eyes.
